widgetData.source_sentence: Why is there a specific requirement for maximum uplink transmission timing difference in asynchronous EN-DC configurations?
sentences: The bandwidth combination set defines the specific arrangements and allowances for channel bandwidths across different NR bands in carrier aggregation configurations. For example, in the case of the CA_n2A-n5A-n48A configuration, the bandwidth combination set is set to 0, indicating that only the specified bandwidths of 5, 10, 15, and 20 MHz can be utilized without any further combinations. This structure ensures that operators can optimize their resources while adhering to technical constraints, leading to improved network efficiency and performance.<|im_end|>

The specific requirement for maximum uplink transmission timing difference in asynchronous EN-DC configurations is crucial for ensuring reliable and efficient communication between the User Equipment (UE) and the network. Variations in timing can lead to synchronization issues, impacting the quality of service and data transmission. By defining these timing differences based on various sub-carrier spacings, the specification aims to optimize the performance of the network while accommodating different configurations, ensuring that the UE can handle the complexities of multi-band operation without significant degradation in service quality.<|im_end|>
2. Integrity protection

widgetData.source_sentence: What factors determine the performance requirement of PUSCH?
sentences: The test aims to verify the behavior of E-UTRAN PCell and NR PSCell during carrier-based switching when transitioning to a carrier that does not support PUCCH/PUSCH transmission. This situation arises when a User Equipment (UE) needs to transmit an aperiodic Sounding Reference Signal (SRS). The test ensures that the system can handle interruptions effectively and maintains connectivity while managing the SRS switching process, which is crucial for maintaining the quality of service in mobile networks.<|im_end|>

For IAB-MT that supports multiple Time Division Duplex (TDD) UL-DL patterns, the testing procedure stipulates that only one of the supported TDD UL-DL patterns should be utilized for all tests associated with a specific subcarrier spacing that is declared to be supported. This requirement simplifies the testing process by ensuring that results are consistent and comparable, as each test will operate under a single defined UL-DL pattern. It is crucial for engineers to select the appropriate UL-DL pattern to ensure that the testing aligns with the declared capabilities of the IAB-MT and maintains the integrity of performance assessments across the defined parameters.<|im_end|>

The performance requirement of the Physical Uplink Shared Channel (PUSCH) is determined primarily by a minimum required throughput, which is influenced by the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in the communication system. This required throughput is quantified as a fraction of the maximum throughput defined for different Fixed Reference Channels (FRCs) listed in annex A of the specification. Additionally, the performance requirements also take into account Hybrid Automatic Repeat reQuest (HARQ) retransmissions, which are vital for ensuring reliable data transmission in environments with variable channel conditions. Thus, both the SNR and the maximum throughput for the specified FRCs are critical in establishing the performance metrics for PUSCH.<|im_end|>

widgetData.source_sentence: What is the mean value of Delay spread at 60° in the Urban LOS scenario at Ka band?
sentences: Table 6.2.2-1: Regional requirements for Band 34 for rated output power declared by the manufacturer.

Table 6.7.2-3b: Channel model parameters for Urban Scenario (LOS) at Ka band The table provides data on various parameters related to urban line of sight (LOS) scenarios. It includes information on delay spread, azimuth spread, angle of departure (AOD), angle of arrival (AOA), zenith spread, shadow fading (SF), Ricean K-factor, cross-correlations, and cluster characteristics. The values are presented for different angles and scenarios. Key keywords include delay spread, azimuth spread, shadow fading, Ricean K-factor, cross-correlations, cluster characteristics, urban LOS, AOD, AOA, zenith spread, mean, standard deviation.

A non-terrestrial network (NTN) faces unique challenges in providing accurate user location information due to the potential coverage of multiple countries by satellite beams. To enforce country-specific policies effectively while a User Equipment (UE) is in RRC CONNECTED mode, a more precise location determination scheme may be required. If the UE cannot report its exact position, such as in cases of GPS failure, the national granularity may be derived from the Public Land Mobile Network (PLMN) of the terrestrial network. Additionally, the UE can report its location through the AMF via NAS messages during the Registration Procedure or through the gNB forwarding the location information upon reception. Implementing these options ensures that the network can apply the appropriate policies based on the user's location.<|im_end|>

widgetData.source_sentence: Which TSG (Technical Support Group) is responsible for interworking with external networks?
sentences: SA WG 1
CT WG 3

widgetData.source_sentence: Which aspects of the repeater’s operation does the input intermodulation requirement apply to?
sentences: The NG_RAN_PRN-Core feature enables User Equipment (UE) to acquire NPN-relevant Cell Global Identity (CGI) information from neighboring intra-frequency or inter-frequency NR NPN cells. This is achieved by the UE reading the System Information (SI) of the neighboring cell and subsequently reporting the acquired CGI information back to the network. The feature is defined in the technical specification TS 38.331, which outlines the procedures and requirements for this capability. Notably, this feature is conditionally mandatory; if the UE supports Non-Public Networks (NPN), it must also support the NG_RAN_PRN-Core feature. This capability is crucial for ensuring that the UE can effectively communicate and interact with NPN environments, enhancing overall network connectivity and performance.<|im_end|>

The input intermodulation requirement applies to both the uplink and downlink operations of the NR repeater. Specifically, if the base station (BS) side is confirmed to meet co-location requirements, it must also adhere to the input intermodulation co-location requirements for the downlink. Similarly, if the user equipment (UE) side meets co-location requirements, it must comply with the input intermodulation co-location requirements for the uplink. This dual applicability ensures comprehensive protection against intermodulation interference.<|im_end|>

The ∆R~IB,c~ values for the inter-band CA configuration CA_n1-n28-n41 are defined specifically for each NR band as part of the specifications. In this case, for band n1, the value is 0 dB; for band n28, the value is 0.2 dB; and for band n41, it is again 0 dB. These values are essential for evaluating the inter-band performance and ensuring proper resource allocation in multi-band operations.<|im_end|>
